nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef flats, slopes, and lagoons from 1 to 30 m depth, typically resting on or encrusting along lower reef surfaces under moderate light and flow.

decorPreferences: sand bed or low rockwork substrate placement
stable flat surface for plating growth
ample open space around colony (10+ cm clearance from neighbors)
avoid burying in fine sand — crushed coral or rubble preferred substrate

diet.notes: The Lithophyllon Coral is primarily photosynthetic, relying on zooxanthellae — symbiotic algae living within its tissue — to produce most of its energy from light. Supplemental target feeding once or twice weekly with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, baby brine shrimp, Reef Roids, or nano-plankton blends tends to support growth and coloration. Foods may be delivered by pipette or turkey baster directly to the coral. Pellets and sinking foods have also been reported as accepted.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer placed nearby
other Lithophyllon without adequate spacing for colony expansion
direct high-flow powerheads aimed at the colony

imageCandidates.notes: Lithophyllon edwardsi by Oceanamazon, 3171x2525. Striking neon-green plating disc with red-tipped tentacles under blue reef lighting — the vivid fluorescent hobbyist look; sharp, whole colony in frame.
Lithophyllon edwardsi by Oceanamazon, 3106x2556. Teal/blue disc with a glowing orange rim under actinic lighting — a second vivid color morph; sharp and vibrant, on a frag rack.
Lithophyllon concinna (as Fungia concinna) by Joi Ito, 5319x3546. Sharp wild in-situ plate colony; correct genus, whole animal framed. Natural muted daylight coloration adds a wild-reef reference.
Lithophyllon undulatum by portioid, 1024x939. Wild in-situ plating colony showing the bowl/plate growth form; correct ID, adds species diversity. Natural brown coloration.

Lithophyllon scabra by Waheed et al. (ZooKeys), 737x546. Wild in-situ specimen adding a fourth distinct species to the genus set; sharp but low-res with a small figure-panel letter, hence filler only.

summary: The Lithophyllon Coral is a genus of plating LPS corals in family Fungiidae, more closely related to free-living disc corals than to the chalice corals it visually resembles. It is generally considered intermediate in care, doing best under moderate light and gentle to moderate flow with placement near the substrate.
careNotes:
Taxonomy note: Despite a plating or encrusting growth form that superficially resembles chalice corals, Lithophyllon belongs to family Fungiidae (order Scleractinia) — the same family as Fungia mushroom and disc corals — rather than Lobophylliidae, where true chalice corals such as Echinophyllia are placed. Lithophyllon is therefore a closer relative of free-living plate corals (Fungia, Cycloseris, Heliofungia) than of the chalice corals it is sometimes mistaken for. In the aquarium trade, Lithophyllon is sometimes marketed under 'chalice coral' labels due to this visual similarity, which can cause confusion at point of sale. The genus comprises six described species; L. undulatum and L. repanda are among those most frequently seen in the hobby, though availability of each varies by region and supplier. Some encrusting multi-mouth specimens previously sold as Echinophyllia 'chalice' may actually be Lithophyllon.

L. repanda is capable of very limited self-repositioning in the wild, occasionally shifting position on the substrate — a trait shared with other Fungiidae members.

Lighting: Generally advised to keep under moderate light, commonly cited around 75–150 PAR at coral depth. Lower to moderate intensity is broadly recommended, with particular care taken when acclimating new arrivals to brighter conditions. New specimens should be started in a shaded area of the tank and gradually moved toward the target position over 2–4 weeks to avoid bleaching. Blue-spectrum lighting tends to enhance fluorescent coloration in color morphs.

Flow: Moderate, indirect flow is broadly recommended. Flow should be sufficient to keep the skeletal surface free of detritus without being strong enough to stress tissue. Sustained laminar flow directed at the colony should be avoided.

Maintaining 5–10 cm minimum spacing from neighboring corals is advisable, with extra clearance allowed as the colony expands.

Placement: Typically placed on or near the sand bed, mirroring the natural resting position of wild Fungiidae corals. A flat, stable surface is ideal; the colony encrusts outward and can plate significantly over time. Chunky rubble or crushed coral substrate is preferred over fine sand, which can accumulate around the base of the colony and cause tissue recession.

Nitrate levels of 5–20 ppm and phosphate under 0.1 ppm are generally considered workable; ultra-low nutrient systems may cause color loss.

Conservation: L. repanda is rated Least Concern by the IUCN; L. undulatum is listed as Near Threatened. The genus is listed on CITES Appendix II, meaning import documentation is required for wild-collected specimens. Most trade specimens are captive-grown frags.

Morph diversity: Lithophyllon is available in a wide range of color morphs in the hobby, including named variants such as C3PO, Immolation, Cheetah, Kiwi, Ice Sheet, and Dragons Blood, among others. These are color morphs of the same genus and do not require different care.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the standard propagation method in captivity. Colonies can be cut with bone cutters, and frags of around 2–3 cm typically encrust and begin growing on rubble or frag plugs within a few weeks under stable conditions. Dipping frags before introduction to a display tank is advisable to prevent hitchhiker pests. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs but is not practiced in home aquaria.

primary.name: AquaLife Support – Lithophyllon Coral
primary.notes: Primary source used for lighting preference (<100 PAR), flow (random gentle), placement (bottom), temperature (74–84°F), salinity (1.024–1.026), feeding (meaty foods), native range (Indian and Pacific oceans, parts of Borneo), and aggression notes (powerful sting, short tentacles).

additional.name: TankFacts – Kiwi Litho (Lithophyllon)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Fungiidae, CITES Appendix II, Near Threatened), placement (bottom), flow (moderate), lighting (moderate), water parameters: temperature 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament. Used as secondary cross-verification.

additional.name: Vivid Aquariums – Strawberry Lithophyllon Coral
additional.notes: Provided water parameters: temperature 75–80°F, salinity 1.024–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, calcium 420–440 ppm, alkalinity 8–9.5 dKH, magnesium 1260–1350 ppm. Listed PAR 250–450 and flow as moderate-to-high — these higher PAR figures appear to be outliers inconsistent with specialist sources and the task brief consensus; the lower PAR range from specialist sources was adopted instead.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Plate Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked Fungiidae taxonomy, Pacific/Australian/Indonesian native range, lighting (50–150 PAR; 50 PAR minimum), flow (medium to low), placement (substrate/bottom), feeding (≤3× weekly, meaty foods), calcium ~425 ppm, alkalinity ~8 dKH, magnesium ~1350 ppm. Describes corals as easy-to-moderate in care; confirmed preferred substrate as medium-chunky rubble/crushed coral over fine sand.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Lithophyllon repanda
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Fungiidae, order Scleractinia), distribution (Indo-Pacific: Gulf of Aden, Red Sea, Indian Ocean, Australia, East China Sea, Japan, western and central Pacific), depth range (1–30 m), colony size (up to 30 cm diameter, up to 23.5 cm maximum recorded), and conservation status (IUCN Least Concern, CITES Appendix II).
